introduction.title: API Reference
introduction.intro: Use the OneUptime API to access any resource in your projects, create automated workflows, and seamlessly integrate with the tools and services your organization uses.
introduction.authBody: Learn how to authenticate your API requests.
introduction.paginationBody: Understand how to work with paginated responses.
introduction.errorsBody: Read about the different types of errors returned.
introduction.permissionsBody: Learn how API Key Permissions work.

authentication.metaDescription: Learn how to authenticate requests with OneUptime API
authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.intro: You'll need to authenticate your requests to access any of the endpoints in the OneUptime API. In this guide, we'll look at how authentication works. OneUptime offers one way to authenticate your API requests - by using an API Key.
authentication.generateApiKeyBody: Please head over to Project Settings > API Keys. Create a new API Key. Please note: New API Keys have no permissions assigned to them, so you will have to assign a permission before you can use it.
authentication.projectIdBody: Please head over to Project Settings > Project. You should see your Project ID there.
authentication.authWithApiKeyBody: You can use OneUptime API Key on Request Header when you're making a request. You can use ApiKey header with your API Key when you make a request.
authentication.warning: Please don't commit your OneUptime API Key to GitHub, or on any other source control project. Please regenerate a new API Key if your API Key is committed by mistake.

pagination.metaDescription: Learn how to paginate requests with OneUptime API
pagination.title: Pagination
pagination.intro: In this guide, we will look at how to work with paginated responses when querying the OneUptime API. By default, all responses limit results to ten.
pagination.body: When an API response returns a list of objects, no matter the amount, pagination is supported. In paginated responses, objects are nested in a data attribute. The API response also has count attribute that indicates total count in the list with that query. You can use the limit and skip query parameters to query pages.
pagination.exampleBody: In this example, we request the list of monitors. As a result, we get a list of three monitors and can tell by the count attribute that we have reached the end of the result set
pagination.limitDescription: Number of items you need to fetch. More items will lead to slower responses. Max limit is 100.
pagination.skipDescription: Number of items to skip. This can be useful when you are paginating items.
pagination.countDescription: Total number of items in the database

permissions.metaDescription: Learn how permissions work with OneUptime
permissions.title: Permissions
permissions.intro: Your API Token needs permissions to create, update, read or delete any resource. If you do not have permissions to make a request a 4xx status will be sent as response. You can manage permissions for your API Key in Project Settings > API Keys.

errors.metaDescription: Learn more about how we return errors from API
errors.title: Errors
errors.intro: In this guide, we will talk about what happens when something goes wrong while you work with the API. Mistakes happen, and mostly they will be yours, not ours. Let's look at some status codes and error types you might encounter.
errors.body: You can tell if your request was successful by checking the status code when receiving an API response. If a response comes back unsuccessful, you can use the status code and error message to figure out what has gone wrong and do some rudimentary debugging (before contacting support).
errors.warning: Before reaching out to support with an error, please be aware that 99% of all reported errors are, in fact, user errors. Therefore, please carefully check your code before contacting OneUptime support.
errors.statusCodesBody: Here is a list of the different categories of status codes returned by the OneUptime API. Use these to understand if a request was successful.
errors.status2xx: A 2xx status code indicates a successful response.
errors.status4xx: A 4xx status code indicates a client error - this means it's a you problem.
errors.status429: Request limit exceeded. Request limits are 100 operations per second per project (this includes all the API keys in the project).
errors.status5xx: A 5xx status code indicates a server error - you won't be seeing a lot of these.
errors.errorMessagesBody: Whenever a request is unsuccessful, the OneUptime API will return an error response with an error message. You can use this information to understand better what has gone wrong and how to fix it. Most of the error messages are pretty helpful and actionable.
errors.errorMessagesExample: Here is an example of an error message:

openapi.metaDescription: Learn more about the OpenAPI specification for OneUptime
openapi.title: OneUptime OpenAPI Specification
openapi.intro: In this guide, we will look at how to import and work with the OneUptime OpenAPI specification. The OpenAPI spec provides a comprehensive reference for all available API endpoints.
openapi.body: The OneUptime API follows the OpenAPI 3.0 specification, which provides a standardized way to describe REST APIs. You can import our OpenAPI spec into various tools like Swagger Editor, Postman, or other API documentation tools to explore and test our endpoints.
openapi.importingBody: To import the OneUptime OpenAPI specification into Swagger Editor, follow these simple steps:
openapi.step1: Open editor.swagger.io in your web browser
openapi.step2: Click on File in the menu bar
openapi.step3: Select Import URL from the dropdown menu
openapi.specUrlDescription: Complete OpenAPI 3.0 specification for the OneUptime API including all endpoints, request/response schemas, and authentication methods.
openapi.benefits: Benefits of Using the OpenAPI Spec
openapi.benefit1Body: Test API endpoints directly in Swagger Editor
openapi.benefit2Body: Generate client SDKs in multiple programming languages
openapi.benefit3Body: Ensure your requests match the expected schema
openapi.benefit4Body: Use with Postman, Insomnia, or other API testing tools

dataTypesGuide.metaDescription: Data Types that can be used to interact with OneUptime API
dataTypesGuide.title: Data Types
dataTypesGuide.intro: In this guide, we will look at how to work with OneUptime Data Types when querying the OneUptime API.
dataTypesGuide.selectBody: Select can be used to select fields from an Object in List or Get Item API. By default only ID's are returned to the client. To retrieve more fields you need use:
dataTypesGuide.selectDescription: Dictionary of fieldName - boolean as JSON Object. Here's an example. If the boolean is set to true then the field will be retrieved.
dataTypesGuide.sortBody: Sort can be used to sort list by fields in List API. By default objects are sorted by their createdAt date in descending order (recently created item will be first). To change the sort order you can:
dataTypesGuide.sortDescription: Dictionary of fieldName - 'DESC' / 'ASC' as JSON Object. Here's an example. If the boolean is set to true then the field will be retrieved.
dataTypesGuide.equalToBody: Here is how to use Equal To query with OneUptime List API:
dataTypesGuide.equalToDescription: Here is an example of an Equal To Query
dataTypesGuide.notEqualToBody: Here is how to use Not Equal To query with OneUptime List API:
dataTypesGuide.notEqualToDescription: Here is an example of a Not Equal To Query
dataTypesGuide.isNullBody: Here is how to use `is null` to query with OneUptime List API:
dataTypesGuide.isNullDescription: Here is an example of an is null query
dataTypesGuide.isNotNullBody: Here is how to use `is not null` to query with OneUptime List API:
dataTypesGuide.isNotNullDescription: Here is an example of an is not null query
dataTypesGuide.greaterThanBody: Here is how to use `greater than` to query with OneUptime List API:
dataTypesGuide.greaterThanDescription: Here is an example of a greater than query
dataTypesGuide.greaterThanOrEqualBody: Here is how to use `greater than or equal` to query with OneUptime List API:
dataTypesGuide.greaterThanOrEqualDescription: Here is an example of a greater than or equal query
dataTypesGuide.lessThanBody: Here is how to use `less than` to query with OneUptime List API:
dataTypesGuide.lessThanDescription: Here is an example of a less than query
dataTypesGuide.lessThanOrEqualBody: Here is how to use `less than or equal` to query with OneUptime List API:
dataTypesGuide.lessThanOrEqualDescription: Here is an example of a less than or equal query
dataTypesGuide.includesBody: Includes will get objects that match any of the values in the array. It is used to filter objects that have a field that matches any of the values in the array. For example, if you want to get all objects that have a label with ID `aaa00000-aaaa-aaaa-aaaa-aaaaaaaaaaaa` or `bbb00000-bbbb-bbbb-bbbb-bbbbbbbbbbbb`, you can use the `includes` query type.
dataTypesGuide.includesDescription: Here is an example of an includes query

model.listIntro: This endpoint allows you to retrieve a paginated list of all your {{title}}. By default, a maximum of ten {{title}} are shown per page.
model.limitDescription: Number of objects to fetch. By default 10, you can increase this count up to 100
model.skipDescription: Number of objects to skip. This can be useful in pagination
model.queryFilterDescription: If you would like to filter on {{title}}. You can specify include a query here. For more information,
model.checkQueriesLink: please check out writing queries here.
model.selectFieldsDescription: By default you will only retrieve ID of objects, to retrieve more fields you need to select them. For more information,
model.checkSelectLink: please check how to select here.
model.sortDescription: Objects will be sorted based on created date by default. You can change the sort order by passing
model.getItemIntro: This endpoint allows you to retrieve {{title}} by ID.
model.countIntro: This endpoint allows you to retrieve the count of all your {{title}}.
model.aggregateIntro: This endpoint runs server-side aggregations (sum, average, count, and percentiles) over your {{title}} and groups the results into time buckets. It powers charts and dashboards.
model.aggregateByDescription: The aggregation to run. Specify aggregationType (Count, Sum, Avg, Min, Max, P50, P90, P95, P99), aggregateColumnName, aggregationTimestampColumnName, startTimestamp, endTimestamp, and an optional query and groupBy. By default the time-bucket size is derived from the window; set the optional aggregationInterval to pin it (Minute, Hour, Day, Week, Month, Year) — for example Day buckets over a one-week window — or to Total to return a single aggregate per group over the whole window with no time bucketing.
model.createIntro: This endpoint allows you to create a new object.
model.updateIntro: This endpoint allows you to update object by its ID.
model.alternativeMethodsBodyUpdate: For clients that do not support PUT requests, you can use POST or GET with the same request headers and body:
model.alternativeMethodsBodyDelete: For clients that do not support DELETE requests, you can use POST or GET with the same request headers and body:
model.deleteIntro: This endpoint allows you to delete object by its ID.
model.permissionsIntro: Your API Token needs permissions to create, update, read or delete this resource. If you do not have permissions to make a request a 4xx status will be sent as response.
model.cannotReadResource: This resource cannot be read via the API
model.cannotCreateResource: This resource cannot be created via the API
model.cannotUpdateResource: This resource cannot be updated via the API
model.cannotDeleteResource: This resource cannot be deleted via the API
model.masterAdminOnlyBody: This API can only be accessed through a Master API Token. You can create one on the Admin Dashboard. Please add the token to the ApiKey header to make the request.

masterAdminApis.metaDescription: Instance health and diagnostics APIs for self-hosted OneUptime, authenticated with the instance master API key.
masterAdminApis.title: Master Admin APIs
masterAdminApis.intro: Read-only health and diagnostics endpoints for the operators who run their own OneUptime instance.
masterAdminApis.body: Every other API in this reference is scoped to a project and authenticated with a project API key. The master admin APIs are different: they describe the instance itself — its datastores, background queues, migrations and logs — and are authenticated with the instance-wide master API key. Call them to monitor the health of your own deployment, or to collect diagnostics before you raise a support request.
masterAdminApis.selfHostedNote: These endpoints only exist on a self-hosted OneUptime instance. The master API key is created by a master admin in your own Admin Dashboard, so there is no equivalent on OneUptime Cloud.
masterAdminApis.masterApiKeyBody: The master API key is an instance-wide key with root access to every resource in every project on your instance. A master admin creates it once:
masterAdminApis.keyStep1: Sign in to your instance as a master admin and go to Admin Dashboard → Settings → API Key.
masterAdminApis.keyStep2: Set Master API Key to a UUID of your choosing. It has to be a valid UUID — any other value is rejected.
masterAdminApis.keyStep3: Turn Master API Key Enabled on. While that toggle is off the key is not accepted, whatever it is set to.
masterAdminApis.keyWarning: The master API key has root access to every project on your instance and is not narrowed by permissions. Treat it like a root password: keep it in a secret manager, never commit it, and turn the toggle off to revoke it instantly.
masterAdminApis.authenticationBody: Send the key in the ApiKey request header. The header name is case-insensitive.
masterAdminApis.authenticationSession: A master admin who is signed in to the Admin Dashboard can also call these endpoints with their session, so the same URLs work from a browser. The key exists for automated callers — a monitoring script or a scheduled job — that have no session. A request with neither a valid key nor a master admin session is rejected with a 422.
masterAdminApis.endpointsBody: Every master admin API is a GET under {{basePath}}, and none of them take query parameters. They are read-only — nothing here changes the state of your instance.
masterAdminApis.overviewDesc: At-a-glance roll-up: whether Postgres, ClickHouse and Redis are reachable, plus a compact background-queue summary. Cached for 15 seconds.
masterAdminApis.queuesDesc: Per-queue background job stats — waiting, active, completed, failed and delayed counts for each of the four work queues. Cached for 15 seconds.
masterAdminApis.failedJobsDesc: The most recent failed jobs for a single queue (up to 25), each with its job body, options, return value, stack trace and per-job logs.
masterAdminApis.clickhouseCapacityDesc: ClickHouse storage capacity: total data size, per-node disk usage, the largest tables, and per-shard local table sizes.
masterAdminApis.clickhouseClusterDesc: ClickHouse cluster health: shard reachability, the distributed DDL queue, replica and replication-queue state, and the Keeper connection. This is what reveals a wedged ON CLUSTER schema sync.
masterAdminApis.clickhouseTelemetryIngestionDesc: Telemetry ingestion throughput — how many log, metric and trace rows landed in ClickHouse over the last minute, hour and day. Useful for spotting a stalled or flooding pipeline.
masterAdminApis.postgresClusterDesc: Postgres cluster health: replication lag, slot health, connection saturation, lock and blocking pressure, cache-hit ratio, and transaction-ID wraparound headroom.
masterAdminApis.redisDesc: Redis connection state and used / max memory.
masterAdminApis.instanceHealthLogsDesc: The 50 most recent instance health events, newest first — for example ClickHouse capacity evaluation and reclamation runs.
masterAdminApis.logsDesc: Diagnostic logs reachable from inside the app process: this instance's own recent log lines, plus what Postgres, ClickHouse and Redis expose. Container stdout is not included — use kubectl logs or docker logs for that.
masterAdminApis.migrationsDesc: Postgres schema and data migration status: whether the instance is fully migrated, what is still pending, and recent failures. This is how you confirm an upgrade finished.
masterAdminApis.supportBundleDesc: One JSON document with everything diagnostic about the instance — version, runtime, redacted config, component health, migrations, datastore diagnostics, schema, failed jobs and logs. The single most useful thing to attach to a support request.
masterAdminApis.queueNamesBody: The {queueName} path parameter has to be one of Workflow, Worker, Telemetry or Runbook. Anything else is rejected with a 400.
masterAdminApis.editionsBody: Most master admin APIs are part of the OneUptime Enterprise Edition, and match the OneUptime Health dashboard they back. On a Community instance they return 402 Payment Required instead of data.
masterAdminApis.editionsCommunityBody: /migrations and /support-bundle are deliberately available on every edition, Community included: confirming your schema is migrated and collecting a support bundle are exactly what you need when an upgrade goes wrong.
masterAdminApis.exampleResponseBody: Each endpoint returns a plain JSON object. The datastore probes fail soft — an unreachable datastore comes back as connected: false with null fields rather than failing the whole request — so one dead component never blanks out the rest of the response.
masterAdminApis.exampleResponseNote: This is the overview endpoint. The other endpoints return their own shapes, and the support bundle nests most of them under one document.
masterAdminApis.sensitiveDataBody: Credentials are scrubbed from every response — config values, connection strings, DDL and sensitive-looking field names. Customer data is a different matter: /queues/{queueName}/failed-jobs and /logs return real job bodies and log lines, and /support-bundle includes both.
masterAdminApis.sensitiveDataWarning: The support bundle is not guaranteed to be free of customer data. Review it before you share it outside your organisation.
masterAdminApis.notAvailable: What the master API key cannot do
masterAdminApis.notAvailableBody: The master admin query console — POST /api/admin/health/query/postgres, /query/clickhouse and /query/redis — runs arbitrary queries against your datastores and is deliberately not reachable with the master API key. Those endpoints need a signed-in master admin session, so that a leaked static key cannot run queries headlessly.
